title: The Three Upāyas: Āṇavopāya, Śāktopāya, and Śāmbhavopāya
subtitle: The hierarchical means to realisation in Abhinavagupta's Tantrāloka, Āhnika 1

summary.one_line: Abhinavagupta's threefold path of liberating knowledge — the Divine Means (Śāmbhavopāya), the Empowered Means (Śāktopāya), and the Individual Means (Āṇavopāya) — as expounded in the first chapter o...
summary.scope: Covers the Śāmbhavopāya (verses 171-213), Śāktopāya (verses 214-218), Āṇavopāya (verses 219-225ab), pratibhā as creative imagination, the 36 tattvas, the Trika triad, and the levels of sound.

sources.label: Tantrāloka of Abhinavagupta, Volume 1 (Āhnika 1), translated by Mark S.G. Dyczkowski

body.text: The three means correspond to the triadic state of the one reality, namely, that of Śiva, Śakti and Nara (the individual soul). Thus, the three means are based on, and lead to, their corresponding states. Each of these is the full and complete triadic reality experienced from one or other of these perspectives, and so each means is based on, and leads to, the same ultimate reality — Anuttara. The Śāmbhavopāya (Divine Means) is based on will (icchā), the Śāktopāya (Empowered Means) on knowledge (jñāna), and the Āṇavopāya (Individual Means) on action (kriyā). (TĀv 1/140-149, pp. 278-289)

The Śāmbhavopāya is the penetration and means pertaining to Śiva. The expression 'one who does not think of anything' implies that in this means thought constructs are of no use. Thus, as they are of no use and do not arise, the attainment of the object of realization is said to be instantaneous. This takes place by means of an extremely intense awakened insight directed at the object of realisation that develops in an instant. It operates in the state of consciousness which is free of thought constructs, pertaining to pure subjectivity free of the contamination of objectivity. (TĀv 1/171-172, pp. 312-322)

The body of the one Lord of Consciousness is His inner creative imagination (pratibhā). It is worshipped externally as the cycle of the waves of the outpouring of the powers of consciousness, in a conditioned limited form as the deity made manifest thereby, whether peaceful or otherwise. The inner creative imagination (āntarī pratibhā) is the power of freedom (svātantryaśakti). The term 'Wave' denotes pratibhā, which means conscious creative energy. This Wave of creative imagination that arises inwardly within consciousness generates configurations — 'wheels' — of divine forms along with their associated Mantras. (TĀv 1/115cd-116ab, pp. 255-259)

Consciousness free of thought constructs (nirvikalpaka) exists. It is variously called in the scriptures 'creative intuition' (pratibhā), 'opening up' (unmeṣa), and is present in the interval between both differentiated conceptual representations — one which has ceased and the other in the process of arising. This Supreme Goddess, creative intuition (pratibhā), is just one alone who, conceived through the glorious power of our forms of language, possesses a limited nature but even so is made of all things. The 36 tattvas from Earth to Śiva are the stages through which consciousness articulates itself as the universe while remaining essentially one. (TĀv 1/186cd-196, pp. 332-342)

The Empowered Means is jñānopāya — the means based on conceptual knowledge (vaikalpikajñāna). Practice here centres on perception (jñāna), that involves working in various ways and degrees with the flux of vikalpa. The Individual Means (Āṇavopāya) operates through the power of action externally as well as internally. The sphere of practice of the Individual Means includes ritual, Yoga, the recitation of Mantra, which are explicitly excluded in the domain of the practice of Empowered Means. The difference between the Empowered and Individual Means is that the former operates through the cognitive power of consciousness and so is internal, while the latter operates through the power of action. (TĀv 1/214-225ab, pp. 357-366)

Abhinava reverses the order in which the three means and their corresponding states of penetration are defined in the MV, because he would like those who practice the teachings to attain the goal as soon as possible. The higher means are more immediate than the lower ones and therefore more effective and efficient. However, which one of them or combination of them operates at a given time for a particular person depends on the intensity of Śiva's grace. It is not a matter of personal choice. All means ultimately culminate in the flash of insight that consciousness cannot be conditioned in any way, because there is nothing outside it. (TĀv 1/228cd-232ab, pp. 368-373)

The levels of sound according to the Netratantra are presented as a hierarchy of Speech: Supreme Speech (Parā Vāc) is the highest, undivided consciousness itself. Then comes the Speech of Vision (Paśyantī), which contemplates each object as a bare trace of consciousness. The Middle Speech (Madhyamā) reflects on the difference between word and referent. Corporeal Speech (Vaikharī) is where the difference between word and referent is fully evident. These four levels correspond to the progressive manifestation of consciousness from its transcendent unity to its fully articulated expression in language and the objective world. (TĀv 1, Appendix A, pp. 441-442)

In reality, Śiva alone, Who is the supreme light of consciousness, is the goal, and He shines everywhere because He does not cease to exist anywhere. Thus, no purpose whatsoever is served by any means to realization, because by definition they make known something that was previously unknown. No Means (Anupāya) is not understood to be just that there is no means to attain the one reality; it is the Divine Being of the Absolute Deity, Who spontaneously reveals Himself in all His fullness as His supreme act of grace. It is Śiva's supreme act of grace that liberates the individual soul without any effort on its part once and for all. (TĀv 1/144-145, pp. 285-289; 1/242-247ab, pp. 377-382)

May that (divine) abode — the Trika reality which is nonduality (itself) and the full (and perfect) realisation of freedom ~ from which (proceed the powers of) will, knowledge and action that are the immanent (udita) planes of (transcendental) consciousness and bliss, and of which all this (universe), which is such, is the (unfolding) glory of its unrestrained power, be (ever) present within my mind, and (thereby) destroy (all that within it) obscures the secret of the transmission (āgamarahasya) of Śiva's teachings. (TĀv 1/1, pp. 2-30)

According to the first etymology, Bhairava's name is derived from the roots 'bhrṛ', meaning to sustain and nourish, and 'ru', to shout. These two roots, along with 'vam', which literally means to vomit, understood metaphorically as 'to emit', are the ones that most commonly appear in the less elaborate etymologies we find in the Tantras and commentaries. (He is called Bhairava because the liberating) reflection on the fear (due to this) fettered existence (bhavabhaya) is rendered lucid by the descent of His power (of grace) (śaktipāta). He is clearly manifest in the minds of those who delight in the contemplation that devours time that (thus) completely exhausts the principle of time that impels the constellations. (TĀv 1/94-105ab, pp. 222-238)

The metaphysical principles (tattva) are the categories into which all the countless individual components of reality and the experience of it can be classified. Travelling upwards, they are the five sensations or subtle elements (tanmātra), the five organs of action, the three inner mental senses: ego (ahaṁkāra), mind (manas), and intellect (buddhi). Above them is Nature (prakṛti) and the individual soul (puruṣa). These twenty-five principles are those of the Sāṁkhya. Āgamic Śaivism adds eleven above them. The thirty-sixth principle beyond that is Śiva, Who is pure transcendental oneness. This is the sphere of nonduality (abheda). All the thirty-six principles, arranged in this sequence and groupings, form a causal chain such that the immediately preceding group is the cause of the succeeding one. This is the reverse of what one would normally think is the case. (TĀv 1/195-196, pp. 341-342)

(The existence) of the gross elements (bhūta) is proved by direct perception, and so they are classified separately from the (other) group of principles (tattva), (whose existence) can only be inferred by (their) effects, (of which they are) the cause (kāryahetu). (The gross Elements are called 'bhūta', meaning literally 'existent', because) an 'existent' is that whose true nature is perceived by everybody, and they know that to be the case here with four (of the five gross elements), along with space (āvakāśa) (as the fifth). The five gross elements are products of sensations, these of the outer senses, these of the inner senses, these of Nature, and that of Individual Soul whose contracted state of consciousness is a result of Māyā and its five attendant principles. Māyā is a product of the higher 'pure' principles. Thus, as the higher principles generate the lower, the upper end of the chain is the ultimate uncaused cause of everything. (TĀv 1/190-194, pp. 336-341)

Right knowledge (samyagjñāna) is the sole cause of liberation, and it is present (both) in oneself (that is, in the teacher) and in the other (that is, in the disciple), because the manifestation (vibhūti) of 'self', 'other' and the like is only imagination (kalpanāmātra). As (the notions of 'self' and 'other') are equally imaginary (kālpanika), the teacher, who is the radiant manifestation of oneness (aikyasphuraṇa) (with the disciples who are one with him), is thus (tāvat) one in nature (with them) and (as such,) is said to be 'accomplished' (siddha) and 'liberated'. A teacher is said to be as great as is his (spiritual) family (saṁtāna), and if (that) is made of right knowledge (samyagjñāna), he therefore liberates himself. A 'spiritual family', which consists of disciples and their disciples etc., is such because, to the degree in which it manifests as being of that nature, it is the manifestation of that one (consciousness which is both teacher and disciple). (TĀv 1/232cd-238, pp. 373-377)

gaps: Full verse-by-verse commentary on the three means still needed
Practical application of the three means in meditation and daily practice needs expansion

issues.title: Limited practical application passages
issues.description: The body passages focus mainly on theoretical exposition. More passages connecting the three upāyas to actual practice would strengthen the RO.

issues.title: Levels of sound section needs expansion
issues.description: Only one passage covers the four levels of sound. More detail on the Netratantra appendix and its connection to the upāyas would be valuable.

issues.title: Etymology of Bhairava section needs more detail
issues.description: The etymology passage covers multiple derivations but does not fully treat all seven etymologies listed by Abhinava.

versions.summary: Initial RO created from Tantrāloka Volume 1, Āhnika 1. Covers the three upāyas, pratibhā, the Trika triad, 36 tattvas, and levels of sound.
Expanded with 5 additional direct passages: the initial invocation verse, etymology of Bhairava, the 36 tattvas description, the five elements, and the teacher and spiritual family.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from Tantrāloka Volume 1
Expanded with passages on invocation, Bhairava etymology, 36 tattvas, five elements, and teacher/spiritual family
